Glossary About
South Asian Traditions

Gautama

c. 2nd c. BCE – 2nd c. CE

The Nyāya-sūtra turns cognition, inference, self, and error into a realist philosophy of mind.

Gautama, more precisely Akṣapāda Gotama or Gautama, is the traditionally credited author or principal compiler of the Nyāya-sūtra, the founding text of the Nyāya school. He should not be confused with Siddhārtha Gautama, the Buddha. The historical dating is uncertain: one contemporary encyclopedia places the oldest extant Nyāya text around c. 200 CE, while another gives a much wider range, from the sixth century BCE to the second century CE. It is safest to treat “Gautama” as the canonical author-function of the early Nyāya system rather than as a biographically recoverable individual. The later tradition develops the sūtras through Vātsyāyana’s Nyāya-bhāṣya, Uddyotakara’s Vārttika, Vācaspati Miśra, Udayana, and eventually Navya-Nyāya with Gaṅgeśa.

The Nyāya-sūtra is not a “philosophy of mind” treatise in the modern departmental sense. It is a work on epistemology, metaphysics, debate, and liberation. Yet a philosophy of mind can be extracted from it with unusual systematicity because Gautama makes cognition, self, body, sense faculties, internal mind, desire, aversion, effort, pleasure, pain, memory, doubt, error, agency, and liberation into explicit objects of philosophical analysis. The result is a realist, pluralist, anti-skeptical, psycho-physical dualism: mental episodes are real qualities; they require a non-material self as their bearer; they are causally dependent on body, sense organs, objects, and an internal mind; and the highest philosophical task is to remove false cognition about the self and thereby end suffering.

1. The project of Nyāya: epistemology as therapy of the mind

The first sūtra announces sixteen topics whose true knowledge leads to the highest good: pramāṇa, prameya, doubt, purpose, example, doctrine, members of inference, hypothetical reasoning, ascertainment, discussion, disputation, destructive debate, fallacy, quibble, futile objection, and defeat. A public English translation renders this as the knowledge of sixteen categories leading to the highest good; the next sūtra gives the soteriological chain: wrong notion produces defect, defect produces activity, activity produces birth, birth produces pain, and the cessation of each later member removes the prior member, culminating in release.

This matters for philosophy of mind because Nyāya does not begin from detached curiosity about consciousness. It begins from the claim that suffering is cognitively structured. Misidentifying the body as the self, taking the temporary as permanent, and mistaking pain-bound pleasure for ultimate good are not merely theoretical errors; they generate affective and motivational distortions. St Andrews’ account of Old Nyāya summarizes the chain as misconception, mental defects such as attachment, aversion, and delusion, activity, repeated birth, and suffering; liberation comes through right cognition of the self, body, mind, and other knowables.

So the first major thesis is this: philosophy of mind is inseparable from epistemic discipline. To understand mind is to understand the instruments by which cognition succeeds or fails, the metaphysical bearer of mental states, the causal structure by which cognitions arise, and the ethical-affective consequences of ignorance.

This makes Nyāya historically distinctive. Western philosophy of mind often begins from questions such as “What is the relation between mind and body?”, “What is consciousness?”, or “What makes a mental state intentional?” Gautama begins with a pramāṇa-centered question: by what reliable means do we know reality, including the self and its mental states? In modern terms, Nyāya’s philosophy of mind is embedded in epistemology, action theory, metaphysics, and moral psychology rather than isolated as a theory of consciousness.

2. The basic mental ontology: self, body, senses, objects, cognition, and mind

The Nyāya-sūtra’s list of knowables is already a map of mind. Gautama enumerates soul or self, body, sense organs, objects, apprehension or cognition, mind, activity, defect, rebirth, fruition, pain, and release as objects of cognition. He then identifies desire, aversion, effort, pleasure, pain, and cognition as “indicatives” or marks of the self. He defines cognition, apprehension, and intellection as synonymous, and he infers mind from the non-appearance of simultaneous cognitions.

The resulting architecture has five core components.

First, ātman, the self, is the enduring subject and substratum of mental qualities. Nyāya argues that desire, aversion, pleasure, pain, effort, and cognition are qualities, and qualities require a substance in which they inhere. Since material elements are not adequate bearers of these mental qualities, Nyāya infers a special non-material substance, the self.

Second, śarīra, the body, is not the self but the embodied site of experience and action. One source summarizes the body as the physical locus that houses pleasure, pain, internal effort, and the sense faculties. The sūtra’s own formulation calls the body the vehicle of actions, sense organs, and objects.

Third, indriyas, the sense faculties, are five external cognitive instruments: olfactory, gustatory, visual, tactile, and auditory. Gautama links their objects to the qualities of the five elements: smell, taste, color, touch, and sound.

Fourth, manas, the internal mind, is a distinct internal organ or faculty. It is not identical with the self. It is also not merely the body. It functions as the internal cognitive channel connecting the self to one sense faculty at a time. The classical Nyāya inference is that if all sense-object contacts could generate cognition simultaneously, we would have many cognitions at once; since we do not, an internal selective organ must mediate cognition.

Fifth, jñāna/buddhi, cognition, is an episodic quality of the self. It is not a substance, not an eternal faculty, and not identical with consciousness as an always-present essence. It is an event-like property that arises under specific causal conditions and presents an object to a cognizer. St Andrews describes Nyāya epistemology as taking cognition to be either recollective, that is memory, or fresh, and as valid when it presents its object as it is.

This gives Nyāya a three-tier model that differs from many Western binaries. It is not simply mind versus body. It is self–mind–body/senses/world. The self is the subject-substance; manas is the internal attentional instrument; the body and senses are external psycho-physical instruments; objects are mind-independent realities; cognitions are qualities produced through their causal coordination.

3. Nyāya dualism: not Cartesian dualism

Nyāya is a dualist tradition, but it is not Cartesian in the strict sense. Modern scholarship on Nyāya philosophy of mind characterizes it as a psycho-physical dualism in which the self is a permanent immaterial substance to which non-physical internal states such as cognition belong; it also emphasizes that Nyāya dualism differs from Cartesian dualism and is not vulnerable to all of the same objections.

The contrast with Descartes is crucial. Cartesian substance dualism treats mind and body as fundamentally distinct substances; the Stanford Encyclopedia describes substance dualism as the view that minds and bodies are distinct kinds of substances, exemplified by Plato, Augustine, and Descartes. Descartes’ mind is essentially a thinking thing. Nyāya’s self is not essentially conscious in that way. Cognition is a quality that arises in the self under appropriate conditions. The self can exist without occurrent cognition, as in deep sleep, and according to many Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika accounts, liberation is a state in which the self is without consciousness. The SEP entry on Indian personhood notes that, for Nyāya, the self is separate from the mind-sense-body complex, depends on that complex for cognition, and loses its distinctive qualities permanently in liberation.

This is perhaps the single most important difference from modern Western assumptions: Nyāya does not identify mentality with consciousness as an intrinsic luminous field. The self is the owner or bearer of cognitions, not an always self-revealing consciousness. The self is metaphysically necessary for mental episodes, but mental episodes are not metaphysically necessary for the self.

This gives Nyāya a strange position from a Western perspective. It is substance-dualist, but not introspection-first. It is realist about mental states, but not consciousness-essentialist. It is anti-materialist, but strongly causal and quasi-naturalistic in its analysis of perceptual production.

4. The inference to the self

The Nyāya argument for selfhood is not primarily a “cogito.” It is an inference from mental qualities to a substratum. Gautama’s sūtra states that desire, aversion, effort, pleasure, pain, and cognition are marks of the self. Commentarial and later Nyāya reasoning develops this as follows: these are qualities; qualities require substances; they are not qualities of earth, water, fire, air, body, or sense organs; therefore they must belong to a non-material self.

This argument’s structure is metaphysical rather than phenomenological. Descartes argues from the indubitability of thinking to the existence of the thinker. Nyāya argues from the ontological dependence of properties to the existence of a property-bearer. The self is not directly perceived in the basic early Nyāya scheme; it is inferred. St Andrews states explicitly that the self cannot be perceived and is inferred from attributes such as cognition, desire, aversion, effort, pleasure, and pain.

Nyāya also argues for the self through cross-modal recognition. If the same object is apprehended by sight and touch, then there must be a single subject capable of synthesizing the deliverances of different sense faculties. Gautama’s text begins the third book with the argument that the same thing is apprehended by sight and touch; the later tradition reads this as evidence for a single experiencer behind multiple sensory channels.

A further argument comes from memory. Recollection presupposes an enduring subject who experienced the earlier event and now remembers it. Gautama states that remembrance is a quality of the soul, and later Nyāya argues that if the present subject were wholly different from the past subject, genuine recollection would be impossible.

This places Nyāya near some Western arguments from unity and memory, but with a different conclusion. Locke associates personhood with reflective consciousness and memory; one SEP entry summarizes Locke’s person as a thinking intelligent being that can consider itself the same thinking thing at different times and places. Nyāya uses memory not to define the person, but to prove an underlying self that persists through memory. Hume’s bundle theory and Buddhist no-self theories are the closer opponents: both deny that mental continuity requires a substantial self. The SEP describes Buddhist theories of mind as centered on not-self, reducing human beings to physical and psychological constituents and processes rather than grounding them in a substantial enduring agent.

So Nyāya’s position can be formulated precisely: memory is epistemic evidence for a persisting self, not the metaphysical criterion of personal identity.

5. Manas as attention, internal sense, and cognitive bottleneck

The doctrine of manas is one of Nyāya’s most sophisticated contributions to philosophy of mind. Gautama infers mind from the non-simultaneity of cognitions. Later discussion clarifies that mind is limited in magnitude and connects with the sense organs one by one, explaining why apprehension is not simultaneous. A sūtra also states that the non-apprehension of one thing is due to the mind being occupied with other things.

This is recognizably an ancient theory of attention. Manas is not “mind” in the broad modern sense of the total mental field. It is closer to an internal attentional organ, central processor, or selective connector. It explains why, even with many sense organs in contact with objects, only one cognition becomes salient at a given moment. St Andrews describes manas as an atomic cognitive channel that connects with one sense faculty at a time, with non-simultaneity proving its existence.

The Nyāya-sūtra also confronts the objection that we seem to perceive several cognitions or actions at once. It replies with an analogy to the fire-circle: rapid motion produces the appearance of simultaneity. This is a strikingly proto-cognitive explanation: apparent simultaneity is an artifact of rapid serial processing.

In modern terms, Nyāya posits a serial attentional bottleneck. Its details are metaphysically alien to contemporary neuroscience, since manas is an atomic internal organ rather than a neural mechanism. But functionally, the idea is recognizable: attention selects, gates, or channels sensory inputs into conscious cognition. Nyāya’s manas is one of the earliest explicit philosophical attempts to explain why sensory availability does not equal conscious apprehension.

6. Perception: direct realism and causal intentionality

Nyāya is a direct realist theory of perception. Gautama defines perception as cognition produced by contact between sense organ and object, not dependent on words, non-erroneous, and determinate or well-defined. The IEP emphasizes that this definition gives Nyāya a direct realist theory: sense-object connection is the central feature of the causal chain that fixes the intentionality of a perceptual cognition.

This is not a representational veil theory. Perception is not primarily awareness of inner ideas from which external objects must be inferred. Rather, when the perceptual causal chain functions properly, the external object itself is presented. Error is not the normal mediation of perception by representations; error is a defective or pseudo-pramāṇa case.

This makes Nyāya sharply different from much early modern Western philosophy, where the “way of ideas” often made the immediate objects of perception mental items. It is closer to certain forms of direct realism and externalist theories of perceptual content. The IEP explicitly notes Nyāya’s epistemology as a sophisticated precursor to contemporary process reliabilism and as granting default entitlement to putatively veridical cognition.

Nyāya also treats perception as conceptually structured, especially in later interpretation. Classical Indian debates asked whether perception is conceptualized or non-conceptual; the SEP notes that Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika and Mīmāṃsā defend a robust realist position about concepts as real properties or universals, in contrast with Buddhist nominalism. Later Nyāya distinguishes indeterminate perception, prior to predicative judgment, from determinate perception such as “this is a cat.”

The comparison with Aristotle is illuminating. Aristotle treats perception in hylomorphic terms as the soul’s perceptive faculty being affected by perceptible forms; perception is a capacity of soul that distinguishes animals from plants. Nyāya converges with Aristotle in taking perception to involve a real causal relation between organism and world, and in rejecting radical subjectivism. It differs because Aristotle’s soul is the form or actuality of the living body, while Nyāya’s self is a distinct non-material substance whose cognitions require bodily and sensory mediation but are not the form of the body.

7. Cognition, validity, and error

Nyāya’s basic mental unit is jñāna, cognition. A cognition presents an object to a subject; when it presents the object as it is, it is valid, and when not, invalid. This is not merely epistemology; it is also a theory of intentional mental content. Cognitions are object-presenting states. They have aboutness.

In modern philosophy, intentionality is the power of minds or mental states to be about, represent, or stand for objects, properties, or states of affairs. Nyāya does not use Brentano’s vocabulary, but it has a comparable concern: a cognition is a state that presents an artha, an object or object-content, to a cognizer. Valid cognition is successful intentionality; error is a misfire in which the presented qualification does not belong to the presented object.

The IEP explains later Nyāya error theory as a mismatch between the substantive targeted by cognition and the property targeted by cognition: in error, a substantive is cognized, but the property does not actually qualify it. This is very close to a structured predication theory of perceptual error. Mistaking a shell for silver is not hallucinating a private object; it is misascribing a property or kind to the wrong locus.

Nyāya also gives doubt a central role. Gautama defines doubt as a wavering judgment in which definite cognition of the specific character of an object is lacking, arising from common features, distinguishing features, conflicting opinions, or uncertain perception and non-perception. Doubt is not the universal starting point of philosophy, as in Descartes. It is a motivated cognitive state that triggers inquiry. The IEP stresses that Nyāya rejects unrestricted skepticism: error and doubt are parasitic on true cognition, and ordinary cognition deserves default trust unless defeated.

This is a major historical contrast. Descartes uses hyperbolic doubt to search for certainty. Nyāya treats global doubt as self-undermining and practically destructive. In modern terms, Nyāya is closer to epistemological externalism and reliabilism than to Cartesian foundationalism.

8. Inference, testimony, and the social mind

Gautama accepts four pramāṇas: perception, inference, analogy, and word or testimony. This has large implications for philosophy of mind. The mind is not epistemically self-sufficient. It knows through perceptual contact, inferential signs, analogical recognition, and reliable linguistic transmission.

Inference is especially important because the self itself is inferred. Nyāya-sūtra 1.1.5 divides inference into cause-to-effect, effect-to-cause, and general-observation-based inference; St Andrews notes that the third kind allows inference to a self that cannot be perceptually checked, from attributes such as desire and aversion.

Testimony is also philosophically significant. Gautama defines “word” as the assertion of a reliable person. St Andrews explains that testimony is valid when a speaker has direct knowledge and the desire to convey it; the hearer thereby gains valid cognition about something not immediately accessible.

This makes Nyāya an early social epistemology of mind. Cognition is not merely private awareness. Human minds are embedded in communicative practices, authority relations, and public standards of reliability. In contrast, much early modern Western philosophy begins with the solitary subject and only later reconstructs the external world and other minds. Nyāya begins from an epistemic community: debate, testimony, defeat conditions, fallacies, and reliable speakers are built into the system from the opening chapter.

9. Memory, recognition, and temporal selfhood

Memory is central to Nyāya’s anti-reductionism about the self. The sūtra states that remembrance is a quality of the soul, and later passages list causes of recollection: attention, association, retention, signs, distinguishing features, likeness, ownership, relation, sequence, separation, pleasure, pain, desire, aversion, fear, need, affection, merit, and demerit.

Several claims are embedded here.

Memory is not fresh perception. It is a recollective cognition. It depends on prior experience, traces or dispositions, associative triggers, and present attention. It belongs to the self but requires manas. This means memory is neither a free-floating mental image nor a purely bodily trace. It is a self-owned cognition causally occasioned by psychological and environmental conditions.

Recognition is also important. Cross-modal recognition shows synchronic unity: the object touched and the object seen are cognized as the same by one subject. Recollection shows diachronic unity: the subject who remembers is linked to the subject who experienced.

The Western comparison with Locke is precise but limited. Locke’s theory makes personal identity deeply tied to consciousness, recollection, and appropriation; the SEP summarizes Locke’s view as defining the self through what it can self-attribute through recollection or appropriation. Nyāya rejects this reduction. For Nyāya, the self grounds memory; memory does not constitute the self. That makes Nyāya closer to Thomas Reid’s later anti-Lockean criticism than to Locke himself, though the metaphysical framework is Indian and not Scottish common-sense philosophy.

10. Emotion, motivation, and affective error

Nyāya’s philosophy of mind includes affect from the start. Desire, aversion, effort, pleasure, and pain are marks of the self. These are not secondary decorations added to cognition. They are central to the causal chain of bondage and liberation.

Gautama defines activity as the operation of speech, mind, and body; defects as having an urging or inciting character; fruition as produced by activity and defect; pain as connected with annoyance; and release as absolute freedom from pain and related conditions. Later in book 4, the defects are grouped under desire, hatred, and illusion, with illusion treated as the deeper evil because desire and hatred do not arise without it.

This is a cognitive-affective theory of suffering. False cognition generates affective distortion; affective distortion generates action; action generates karmic consequences; consequences generate further embodied suffering. The therapy is not emotional suppression alone, but true cognition.

Nyāya’s analysis of pleasure is severe. The sūtra tradition argues that birth and embodiment are pain because they are beset by annoyances; the objection that pleasure occurs in intervals is answered by saying that the person enjoying pleasure remains oppressed by longing, and that many pains are wrongly regarded as pleasures. The mental life of ordinary agents is therefore structured by misvaluation: we mistake unstable, dependence-producing states for goods.

This differs from much Greek eudaimonism, where the good life can involve rationally ordered pleasure or flourishing in embodied life. Nyāya is closer to Buddhist and other Indian soteriologies in treating ordinary pleasure as bound to suffering, but it rejects Buddhist no-self and grounds liberation in knowledge of a real self.

11. Agency: speech, mind, body, and karmic consequence

Nyāya has a tripartite theory of action: activity occurs through speech, mind, and body. This is important because it refuses to reduce action to bodily motion. Mental acts and verbal acts are morally and causally significant. The subject is an embodied self whose inner and outer operations produce consequences.

Purpose also plays a role. Gautama defines motive or purpose as the object aiming at which one acts. Desire and aversion orient the subject toward pursuit and avoidance. Effort translates affective orientation into action. The mind is therefore not merely representational; it is conative and practical.

Yet there is a tension. If the pure self is only a bearer of qualities and depends on mind-sense-body contact for cognition, can it be a genuine agent? The SEP entry on personhood in Indian philosophy presses this worry: the Nyāya self, considered apart from the mind-sense-body complex, seems causally impotent; it is the embodied self or person, not the bare self, that functions as agent, doer, cognizer, and enjoyer.

This is one of the deepest problems in Nyāya philosophy of mind. Its metaphysics wants a pure self distinct from body and mind. Its action theory requires an embodied cognitive agent. The best reconstruction is that agency belongs to the self only as embodied and connected with manas, sense organs, body, and motivational qualities. A bare liberated self may persist, but it no longer acts or cognizes in the ordinary sense.

12. Self-consciousness and second-order awareness

The Nyāya-sūtra does not present a full-blown modern theory of self-consciousness, but later Nyāya develops one consistent with the sūtra’s architecture. Since cognition is a quality of the self, and since cognition is not self-luminous, awareness of a cognition requires another cognition. The IEP notes that in apperception, the relevant causal chain involves the internal organ and an immediately prior cognition.

This becomes a major contrast with Advaita Vedānta and Yogācāra Buddhism. A modern article on consciousness and meditation summarizes the debate: Nyāya held that no conscious act is conscious of itself, just as a knife cannot cut itself; it becomes conscious only by becoming the object of another act of consciousness. Vedāntins and Yogācāra Buddhists replied with the light analogy: consciousness reveals itself while revealing objects.

In Western terms, Nyāya rejects strong self-luminosity and is closer to higher-order or inner-perception accounts of consciousness. It does not think that every conscious state is intrinsically self-aware. This puts it at odds with many phenomenological theories of pre-reflective self-awareness, but close to theories that distinguish first-order object cognition from second-order awareness of that cognition.

This also clarifies why Nyāya is not Cartesian. Descartes begins from the apparent transparency of thinking to itself. Nyāya treats cognition as knowable, but not automatically self-certified merely by occurring.

13. Language, concepts, universals, and mental content

Nyāya’s philosophy of mind is also a philosophy of conceptual cognition. In the linguistic section, Gautama considers whether words denote individuals, configurations, or generalities. The conclusion is that individual, configuration, and generality together constitute the denotation of a word; generality is the cause or basis of comprehensive cognitions.

This matters because Nyāya is realist about universals. When one cognizes a cow as a cow, the cognition is not merely a private association or linguistic habit. It tracks a real universal, cowhood, instantiated in individual cows. The SEP’s entry on perception in classical Indian philosophy notes that Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika defends robust realism about concepts as real properties, essences, or universals, whereas Buddhist schools tend toward nominalism.

This gives Nyāya a non-nominalist theory of intentional content. General concepts are not merely mental constructs; they are grounded in real repeatable features of the world. In Western comparison, Nyāya is closer to Aristotelian or scholastic realism about universals than to Humean associationism or nominalism.

14. The body and the rejection of materialism

Nyāya argues that sentience is not a quality of the body. The sūtras consider and reject the idea that consciousness belongs to the body, partly because bodily qualities such as color are perceptible by the senses and persist in characteristic ways, while sentience differs from such bodily qualities. The text also argues that mind must be one because cognitions are non-simultaneous and that apparent simultaneity is due to rapidity, like the perception of a moving firebrand as a circle.

These arguments are directed against materialist or quasi-materialist positions such as Cārvāka, and also against views identifying cognition with body, senses, or mind alone. Their enduring philosophical point is not that Nyāya has a modern neuroscience-proof argument against physicalism. Rather, Nyāya is asking: what is the proper bearer of mental qualities, and can body, senses, or internal organ alone account for memory, cross-modal unity, pleasure, pain, desire, and moral continuity?

From a contemporary perspective, Nyāya’s anti-materialist arguments are not decisive. Neuroscience can explain many correlations between brain, body, attention, and conscious states in ways unavailable to Gautama. But Nyāya’s objections remain philosophically relevant where they press issues of subject-unity, first-person ownership, memory, intentionality, and the irreducibility of normative cognition to mere bodily motion.

15. Liberation: the final transformation of mind

Nyāya’s final aim is liberation, defined negatively as the cessation of suffering. The sūtra defines final release as absolute freedom from pain and related conditions. St Andrews states that Nyāya’s definition of liberation is negative: riddance or absolute absence of suffering.

This is not a mystical absorption into a universal consciousness. Nyāya is pluralist: selves remain distinct. Nor is it the Buddhist realization that there is no self. Nyāya liberation follows from true cognition of the self and other realities. False identification gives rise to desire, aversion, and action; true knowledge destroys the cause of the defects. Gautama says that true knowledge of the cause of defects leads to cessation of the notion of “I” in the relevant erroneous sense, and that desire and the rest have their source in misapprehension.

The phrase “cessation of the notion of I” must be read carefully. Nyāya does not deny the self. It denies false egoic appropriation: mistaking body, pleasures, possessions, and transient conditions for the self. Unlike Buddhism, the cure for egoic misidentification is not no-self, but correct self-knowledge.

This yields a paradoxical result: Nyāya defends the self in order to transcend ordinary egoity. Its philosophy of mind is therefore both realist and ascetic. The self is real, but most of what we call “myself” in ordinary life is a mistaken complex of body, affect, memory, pleasure, possession, and desire.

16. Historical position in the global development of philosophy of mind

Historically, Gautama’s Nyāya-sūtra belongs to a period in which Indian philosophers were building rigorous systems of debate, epistemology, ontology, and liberation. Nyāya’s prehistory is tied to traditions of debate and rules of reasoning; the school later becomes one of the most important opponents of Buddhist anti-realism and no-self theory.

In the Indian context, Nyāya’s philosophy of mind is defined by several polemical fronts. Against Buddhists, it defends enduring substances and selves. Against materialists, it denies that consciousness is merely a bodily property. Against Advaita-like self-luminosity views, later Nyāya denies that consciousness is intrinsically self-revealing. Against skepticism, it defends pramāṇas as reliable by default. Against purely scriptural approaches, it gives reasoning and evidence a central role.

In the Western chronology, if the Nyāya-sūtra is placed around the early centuries CE, it comes after Plato and Aristotle, roughly before or around late ancient and early Christian developments, and long before Descartes. Conceptually, however, it does not fit a simple “ancient West versus ancient India” contrast.

Compared with Plato, Nyāya shares a commitment to a non-material self and to the superiority of rational knowledge over bodily confusion, but it lacks Plato’s theory of Forms and instead develops a realist ontology of substances, qualities, universals, inherence, and epistemic instruments.

Compared with Aristotle, Nyāya shares realism, attention to sense faculties, and a causal theory of perception. But Aristotle’s soul is the form of a living body, whereas Nyāya’s self is a distinct substance whose cognition depends on body and mind without being identical to bodily form.

Compared with Descartes, Nyāya shares substance dualism and an immaterial self. But Descartes makes thinking essential to mind; Nyāya treats cognition as an adventitious quality of the self produced under causal conditions. Descartes begins from first-person certainty; Nyāya begins from public pramāṇa-theory and inference.

Compared with Locke, Nyāya treats memory as crucial to personal continuity, but it refuses to reduce personal identity to memory or consciousness. Memory proves the self; it does not constitute it.

Compared with Hume, Nyāya is almost the opposite. Humean bundle theory says the self is nothing over and above changing experiences; Nyāya says changing experiences require an enduring substratum. The SEP notes that Hume takes the self to be a constantly varying bundle of experiences, while Locke ties the self to recollection and appropriation.

Compared with Brentano and modern intentionality theory, Nyāya has a robust account of object-directed cognition, but it embeds intentionality in epistemic success, pramāṇa, and realist ontology rather than treating intentionality simply as the mark of the mental. Modern intentionality theory asks how mental states can be about objects; Nyāya answers through causal contact, universals, valid cognition, and error-analysis.

Compared with functionalism, Nyāya is not physicalist or computational. Yet some of its analyses are functional in a broad sense: manas is individuated partly by its role in gating cognition; desire, aversion, effort, pleasure, and pain are placed in causal relations to action; pramāṇas are reliable processes producing true cognition. Functionalism in contemporary philosophy defines mental states by their causal roles among sensory stimulations, other mental states, and behavior; Nyāya does something structurally similar while retaining a non-material self-substratum.

17. A reconstructed Nyāya philosophy of mind in theses

A comprehensive reconstruction of Gautama’s philosophy of mind can be stated as follows.

The mind-independent world exists and is knowable. Cognition is not sealed inside representations. The external object, when properly connected with the relevant sense faculty and mediated by manas, can be directly known.

Cognition is an episodic quality, not a substance. It belongs to a self and presents an object. Cognitions divide into fresh cognitions and memories, and into valid and invalid cognitions.

The self is a real, enduring, non-material substance inferred from mental qualities such as cognition, desire, aversion, effort, pleasure, and pain. The self is not identical with body, sense organs, mind, or a stream of momentary mental events.

The body is necessary for ordinary experience and action, but it is not the self. It is the vehicle of action, sense faculties, and contact with objects.

The five external senses are object-specific instruments. They are coordinated by manas, the internal organ. Manas explains attention, serial cognition, and the absence of simultaneous apprehension.

Mental life includes cognition, affect, conation, and volition. Desire, aversion, effort, pleasure, and pain are not reducible to cognition, but they are linked to cognition through error, valuation, and action.

Error is not basic. Valid cognition is the norm; doubt and error are intelligible only against a background of successful cognition. This makes Nyāya anti-skeptical and reliabilist in spirit.

Memory and cross-modal recognition support the unity and persistence of the subject. They are not merely psychological continuities; they are evidence for an underlying self that owns them.

Self-consciousness is not intrinsic self-luminosity. A cognition is known by a further cognition. This makes Nyāya a higher-order or apperceptive theory rather than a luminosity theory.

The mind is normatively and ethically consequential. Wrong cognition produces defects; defects produce action; action produces suffering. Right cognition removes the causal basis of bondage.

Liberation is not maximal conscious bliss in the Vedāntic sense, nor no-self in the Buddhist sense. It is the cessation of suffering through the destruction of ignorance and its affective-conative effects.

18. Strengths and problems of the Nyāya model

The strengths are substantial.

Nyāya offers a sophisticated account of the unity of consciousness. Cross-modal recognition, memory, and ownership of mental qualities are used to argue that cognition requires more than disconnected sensory events.

It gives an early theory of attention through manas. Non-simultaneity, occupation of mind, and apparent simultaneity through rapid succession are philosophically important, even if Nyāya’s metaphysical explanation is not scientifically modern.

It integrates epistemology and philosophy of mind better than many traditions. Perception, inference, testimony, doubt, error, memory, and selfhood are parts of a single theory.

It avoids a crude Cartesian theater. Cognition is not a private inner object inspected by a ghostly ego. It is a causally produced quality of a self, involving object, senses, mind, and world.

It has a rich moral psychology. Desire, aversion, delusion, pleasure, pain, effort, and action are all placed into a causal structure of suffering and liberation.

The problems are equally important.

The inference from mental qualities to a non-material self depends on Nyāya’s substance-property ontology. A critic who rejects that ontology will not be compelled by the argument. Buddhist and Humean-style bundle theorists can say that mental events are interrelated without requiring a substratum.

The bare self risks becoming too thin. If the self is not intrinsically conscious and depends on manas-body-sense contact for cognition, it is difficult to explain how it is genuinely the subject rather than an inert bearer. The SEP’s personhood discussion presses exactly this worry: the bare Nyāya self can seem causally impotent, with real agency belonging to the embodied person.

The theory of liberation as consciousness-free cessation is hard to reconcile with traditions that treat consciousness as intrinsically valuable. It is also distant from much contemporary philosophy of mind, which tends to treat consciousness as central to mentality.

The manas theory explains serial attention but struggles with the phenomenology of simultaneous multimodal experience. Nyāya replies with rapid succession, but contemporary cognitive science would require a much more complex model.

Finally, some details of the perceptual theory, such as ancient accounts of visual contact, are scientifically obsolete. But the philosophical core—direct realism, causal intentionality, attentional mediation, and error as misqualification—remains intellectually alive.

19. Final synthesis

Gautama’s Nyāya-sūtra yields one of the world’s earliest rigorous philosophies of mind. Its central claim is not simply “there is a soul.” It is a structured theory of how selves know, misperceive, desire, suffer, act, remember, infer, and become free.

Its deepest originality lies in the combination of four commitments: realism about the external world; inferential realism about the self; causal analysis of cognition through object, sense, manas, and self; and soteriological analysis of suffering as rooted in false cognition. It is dualist, but not Cartesian; empirically oriented, but not materialist; rationalist in method, but not a priori in the Western sense; anti-skeptical, but not naïve; ascetic, but not no-self.

In the global history of philosophy of mind, Nyāya deserves to be placed alongside Aristotle’s psychology, Buddhist no-self theories, Cartesian dualism, Lockean memory theory, Humean bundle theory, and contemporary debates over intentionality, attention, self-consciousness, and mental causation. Its distinctive contribution is the self–manas–body–world model: a layered account in which consciousness is neither a bodily secretion nor an intrinsically luminous absolute, but an episodic quality of an enduring self produced through reliable or defective cognitive instruments.

The result is a philosophy of mind whose guiding question is not merely “What is mind?” but “What must the mind, self, and world be like for knowledge, error, agency, suffering, and liberation to be possible?”

PreviousUpaniṣads NextNyāya

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
